posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Wine dinner with old non-wino (but wino-curious) friends.

NV Agrapart BdB Champagne “Terroirs”

This bottle has been in my cellar for 3-4 years, and I really liked it. It had a nice, persistent, yeasty note that I really dug.

1994 Hermann Ludes Thornischer Ritsch Riesling Spätlese. This was the AP 3/96 bottling. Almost zero friction between bottle and cork, and cork was also rather hard on top, and as such just the slightest pressure from the tip of the Durand worm pushed it down and I couldn’t get even the point to penetrate and “catch”, so into the bottle it went. That had me worried, but the wine was delicious. Open, expressive, still showing a fair amount of sweetness. Terrific.

2004 Ch. Smith-Haut-Lafitte blanc. I’m not big on most white Bordeaux because I don’t care for typical sauvignon blanc flavors. The good news is that this one didn’t hit me with any of those flavors, but OTOH it is showing some oxidized/sherry/nutty flavors that also aren’t my bag. At the same time, this was very popular around the table with many folks taking pics of the label for their next trip to the wine shop. So there you go.

With the main course (roast rack of lamb and potatoes au gratin), we had 4 Pauillacs single blind. All were double-decanted (returning them immediately to the open bottle) about 3 hours before being served.

My expectation based on reputation was that the legendary '89 Lynch Bages would outclass the field, the '95 Pichon Lalande would shine, and the '95 Pontet-Canet and Grand-Puy-Lacoste would be tasty but not in the same league as the other two. Blind tasting can defy such expectations, of course, and did so again. All were generally just as you’d expect from a mature but not yet tertiary Pauillac. (I’d have hoped for more tertiary notes, especially from the now 32 year old LB). What distinguished one from another to me was relatively minor, but the wine that turned out to be the LB had a slight sour note I didn’t care for, the Pichon Lalande just seemed to have more of everything, and the other two had no off-notes, but a little less of everything than the PLL. Of the tasters who ranked them by preference, 1 had the Pontet Canet at the top, but 4 or 5 of us ranked them as PLL best, GPL next, P-C third, and LB last. At least these bottles on this night… I would classify the LB as a disappointment relative to expectations/reputation/price, with the other three more as expected.

1988 Rabaud-Promis Sauternes to finish was a delight and just what you’d want from a mature sticky.

posts.name: Jim Cowan
posts.cooked:
I’ve just gotten acquainted with Agrapart (thanks to Nathan); also had the Terroirs and liked it.
Many years ago (close to release) I found the ‘89 Lynch on sale and bought a lot. I have since gone through it all but it surprises me that there’s nothing tertiary noticeable. It was always good but never great in the times I had it.
These days, Bordeaux often confuses me. Not that I don’t enjoy it but many of the house styles seem more modern. I don’t drink enough of it to worry about that but, when I do have a glass or two, I seldom find a house that has the same style I remember.
Best, Jim

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked: Hmmm… had an 89 LB last month that was ethereal. Maybe my woty. Friend bought on release and stored perfectly himself so perhaps that made the difference

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Hmmm… had an 89 LB last month that was ethereal. Maybe my woty. Friend bought on release and stored perfectly himself so perhaps that made the difference

I’ve stored mine perfectly myself for quite a long time but it wasn’t bought on release. Bought at local retail maybe 3 years after release? But at that age “only great bottles,” etc. so maybe it was shipping or storage before I bought it or maybe it was one of those bottles that just doesn’t sing.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Too bad about your bottle of the Lynch! I opened one during harvest and it was one of the best Bordeaux I drank all year: still an infant, immensely concentrated, and so vibrant. For me, probably the best wine of the Médoc in 1989.

1995 Pontet Canet around the same period I found rather blocky and simple, but perfectly nice.

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked:
Hmmm… had an 89 LB last month that was ethereal. Maybe my woty. Friend bought on release and stored perfectly himself so perhaps that made the difference

I’ve stored mine perfectly myself for quite a long time but it wasn’t bought on release. Bought at local retail maybe 3 years after release? But at that age “only great bottles,” etc. so maybe it was shipping or storage before I bought it or maybe it was one of those bottles that just doesn’t sing.

I suspect the other bottles in the line up had some impact as well. In my case some champagne, a few 7-10 yr old CA pinot and chardonnay, some chablis and some Brunello but no other BDX or anything close to that old in the line up

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
I’d say that sour note on the 1989 Lynch Bages somewhat killed it for you.

In spite of that, not including the out-of-this-world Haut Brion and LMHB, I’m one of few who’d put the Lynch Bages behind a clean bottle Montrose, La Conseillante and the Palmer, in a great Bordeaux vintage of 1989.

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked: That '94 Ludes bottle has been a fun ride the last few years. Great deals on those ex cellar through Fass. Nice notes on a cool little lineup.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
Hmmm… had an 89 LB last month that was ethereal. Maybe my woty. Friend bought on release and stored perfectly himself so perhaps that made the difference

I’ve stored mine perfectly myself for quite a long time but it wasn’t bought on release. Bought at local retail maybe 3 years after release? But at that age “only great bottles,” etc. so maybe it was shipping or storage before I bought it or maybe it was one of those bottles that just doesn’t sing.

We have had the 1989 this year and it was similar to David’s experience. Same with prior bottles.

We only have on bottle remaining, so I will report someday!

Dave, very sorry that bottle was unimpressive. No telling what can happen.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
What is your hit rate with non-horsey Montrose? I’ll take Las Cases and Lynch Bages over Montrose in ‘89 and most vintages. I HATE brett.

2 out of 4 (or 3 out of 5). Yep, not a good batting average, but always a homerun when it connects cleanly.
In one all 1989 local NYC tasting that I documented in this board, the Pichon Baron and P Lalande even upstaged the Lynch Bages in the same Pauillac flight.
The 89 Montrose and Palmer were voted tied WOTN. LLC was 2nd place WOTN.

In another instance where we drank a Montrose vertical, the 1989 Montrose stood toe-to-toe with the 1990, both clean bottles, for WOTN honors. The 1990 took it.
